[Ranuccio I Farnese, Duke of Parma](/wiki/Ranuccio_I_Farnese,_Duke_of_Parma "Ranuccio I Farnese, Duke of Parma") [Margherita, Hereditary Princess of Mantua](/wiki/Margherita_Farnese "Margherita Farnese") [Cardinal Oddoardo](/wiki/Odoardo_Farnese_(cardinal) "Odoardo Farnese (cardinal)")

([Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"): *Alessandro Farnese*, [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"): *Alejandro Farnesio*; 27 August 1545 – 3 December 1592) was an Italian [noble](/wiki/Nobility "Nobility") and military leader, who was [Duke of Parma, Piacenza](/wiki/Duke_of_Parma_and_Piacenza "Duke of Parma and Piacenza") and [Castro](/wiki/Duchy_of_Castro "Duchy of Castro") from 1586 to 1592, as well as [Governor of the Spanish Netherlands](/wiki/Governor_of_the_Spanish_Netherlands "Governor of the Spanish Netherlands") from 1578 to 1592. Nephew to King [Philip II of Spain](/wiki/Philip_II_of_Spain "Philip II of Spain"), he served in the [Battle of Lepanto](/wiki/Battle_of_Lepanto "Battle of Lepanto") and the subsequent campaigns of the [Holy League](/wiki/Holy_League_(1571) "Holy League (1571)") against the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"). He was latter appointed general of the Spanish army during the [Dutch revolt](/wiki/Dutch_revolt "Dutch revolt") and its ramifications, serving in [Netherlands](/wiki/Habsburg_Netherlands "Habsburg Netherlands"), [France](/wiki/Kingdom_of_France "Kingdom of France") and the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") until his death in 1592.

**Alexander Farnese** ([Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"): *Alessandro Farnese*, [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"): *Alejandro Farnesio*; 27 August 1545 – 3 December 1592) was an Italian [noble](/wiki/Nobility "Nobility") and military leader, who was [Duke of Parma, Piacenza](/wiki/Duke_of_Parma_and_Piacenza "Duke of Parma and Piacenza") and [Castro](/wiki/Duchy_of_Castro "Duchy of Castro") from 1586 to 1592, as well as [Governor of the Spanish Netherlands](/wiki/Governor_of_the_Spanish_Netherlands "Governor of the Spanish Netherlands") from 1578 to 1592. Nephew to King [Philip II of Spain](/wiki/Philip_II_of_Spain "Philip II of Spain"), he served in the [Battle of Lepanto](/wiki/Battle_of_Lepanto "Battle of Lepanto") and the subsequent campaigns of the [Holy League](/wiki/Holy_League_(1571) "Holy League (1571)") against the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"). He was latter appointed general of the Spanish army during the [Dutch revolt](/wiki/Dutch_revolt "Dutch revolt") and its ramifications, serving in [Netherlands](/wiki/Habsburg_Netherlands "Habsburg Netherlands"), [France](/wiki/Kingdom_of_France "Kingdom of France") and the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") until his death in 1592.

Farnese was regarded by contemporaries and historians as the greatest general of his age, as well as one of the best in history, for his talents as a commander, strategist and diplomat.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECreasy1851351-1) He held a high leadership over the soldiers and mercenaries of varied nationalities which composed the Spanish *[tercios](/wiki/Tercio "Tercio")*, including Spaniards, Italians, Germans and Walloons.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGriffin2012128-2) His campaigns marked Spain's greatest successes in the [Eighty Years' War](/wiki/Eighty_Years%27_War "Eighty Years' War"), giving the Spanish crown permanent control of the southern provinces, in the process establishing the cultural and religious separation which would eventually become the nation of [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium").[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Groof1993195-219-3)

During the [French Wars of Religion](/wiki/French_Wars_of_Religion "French Wars of Religion"), he decisively [relieved Paris](/wiki/Siege_of_Paris_(1590) "Siege of Paris (1590)") for the [Catholic League](/wiki/Catholic_League_(French) "Catholic League (French)"),[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Groof1993195-219-3) and also intervened in the [Cologne War](/wiki/Cologne_War "Cologne War"), granting victory to [Ernest of Bavaria](/wiki/Ernest_of_Bavaria "Ernest of Bavaria"). He was slated to direct the land invasion of the [British isles](/wiki/British_Isles "British Isles") in the maligned 1588 [Spanish Armada](/wiki/Spanish_Armada "Spanish Armada"), which failed to embark him and his [Army of Flanders](/wiki/Army_of_Flanders "Army of Flanders"). British historian [Edward Shepherd Creasy](/wiki/Edward_Shepherd_Creasy "Edward Shepherd Creasy") wrote about him: "happy it is for England and the world that this island was saved from becoming an arena for the exhibition of his powers".[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECreasy1851351-1)

## Early life: 1545–1577

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alexander_Farnese,_Duke_of_Parma&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life: 1545–1577")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Alessandro_Farnese%2C_S._Anguisciola.jpg/250px-Alessandro_Farnese%2C_S._Anguisciola.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alessandro_Farnese,_S._Anguisciola.jpg)

Portrait of the Prince when he was 15 by [Sofonisba Anguissola](/wiki/Sofonisba_Anguissola "Sofonisba Anguissola"), 1560

Alessandro, born 27 August 1545, was the son of Duke [Ottavio Farnese](/wiki/Ottavio_Farnese "Ottavio Farnese") of Parma (a grandson of [Pope Paul III](/wiki/Pope_Paul_III "Pope Paul III")) and [Margaret of Austria](/wiki/Margaret_of_Austria_(1522-1583) "Margaret of Austria (1522-1583)") (an illegitimate daughter of the Holy Roman Emperor and king of Spain [Charles V](/wiki/Charles_V,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Charles V, Holy Roman Emperor")).[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020av. I_p. 10-4) He had a twin brother, Carlo, who died in Rome on 7 October 1549.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2021 Addendum_pp. 227–228-5) He and his mother, the half-sister of [Philip II of Spain](/wiki/Philip_II_of_Spain "Philip II of Spain") and [Don Juan](/wiki/John_of_Austria "John of Austria"), left Rome for Parma in 1550. When Margaret was appointed [Governor of the Netherlands](/wiki/Governors_of_the_Habsburg_Netherlands "Governors of the Habsburg Netherlands"), Alessandro accompanied her to [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels") in 1556 and was delivered to Philip II to ensure the loyalty of the Farnese.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020av. I_pp. 20–25-6) While in the King's custody, he visited the English royal court and then went to Spain to be raised and educated with his cousin, the ill-fated [Don Carlos](/wiki/Carlos,_Prince_of_Asturias "Carlos, Prince of Asturias") and his half-uncle, [Don Juan](/wiki/John_of_Austria "John of Austria"), both of whom were about the same age as himself.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChisholm1911184-7)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Coat_of_Arms_of_Alessandro_Farnese%2C_Duke_of_Parma_%281585-1592%29.svg/250px-Coat_of_Arms_of_Alessandro_Farnese%2C_Duke_of_Parma_%281585-1592%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Coat_of_Arms_of_Alessandro_Farnese,_Duke_of_Parma_(1585-1592).svg)

Coat of Arms of the Farnese as dukes of Parma.

In 1565 his marriage with [Maria of Portugal](/wiki/Maria_of_Portugal_(1538-1577) "Maria of Portugal (1538-1577)"), which ended his stint as Philip II's hostage, was celebrated in [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels") with great splendour.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020av. I_pp. 103–140-8) He commanded three galleys during the [Battle of Lepanto (1571)](/wiki/Battle_of_Lepanto_(1571) "Battle of Lepanto (1571)") and the subsequent campaigns against the Turks.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020av. I_pp. 160–184-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) It was seven years before he again had the opportunity to display his great military talents. During that time the provinces of the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") had revolted against Spanish rule. Don Juan, who had been sent as governor-general to restore order, found difficulties in dealing with [William the Silent](/wiki/William_I_of_Orange "William I of Orange"), who had succeeded in uniting all the provinces in common resistance to [King Philip II](/wiki/Philip_II_of_Spain "Philip II of Spain").[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChisholm1911184-7)

## Governor-General of the Netherlands

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alexander_Farnese,_Duke_of_Parma&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Governor-General of the Netherlands")]

Further information: [Eighty Years' War, 1579–1588](/wiki/Eighty_Years%27_War,_1579%E2%80%931588 "Eighty Years' War, 1579–1588")

In the autumn of 1577 shortly after his wife's death, Farnese led Spanish reinforcements from Italy along the [Spanish Road](/wiki/Spanish_Road "Spanish Road") to join Don Juan, and it was his able strategy and prompt decision at a critical moment that decisively won the [Battle of Gembloux](/wiki/Battle_of_Gembloux_(1578) "Battle of Gembloux (1578)") in early 1578.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020av. I_pp. 210–218-12) Shortly thereafter was the [Siege of Zichem (Dutch)](https://nl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Beleg_van_Zichem "nl:Beleg van Zichem") where the garrison was put to the sword and the town was sacked. This incident is considered to be the biggest stain on Farnese's otherwise chivalrous career.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020av. I_pp. 223–233-13) Next was the five-day [Siege of Nivelles (Dutch)](https://nl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Beleg_van_Nijvel "nl:Beleg van Nijvel") [(French)](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Si%C3%A8ge_de_Nivelles "fr:Siège de Nivelles") in March 1578. The example of Zichem encouraged the citizens to capitulate so quickly. That summer, Farnese managed to prevent the defeat at the [Battle of Rijmenam](/wiki/Battle_of_Rijmenam_(1578) "Battle of Rijmenam (1578)") from being decisive.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020av. I_pp. 272–277-14)

In October 1578, Don Juan, whose health had broken down, died.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChisholm1911184-7) Phillip II appointed Farnese to take his place as [Captain-General](/wiki/Captain-General "Captain-General") of the [Army of Flanders](/wiki/Army_of_Flanders "Army of Flanders") and appointed his mother Margaret as Governor-General. This was unacceptable to Alexander; he demanded to be both Captain-General and Governor-General or he would resign thus leaving military matters entirely in Margaret's hands. Philip eventually capitulated and after four years, Margaret returned to Parma.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020bv. II_pp. 322–346-15)

Upon Don Juan's death, Farnese was confronted with a difficult situation. Perceiving that his opponents were divided between [Catholic and Protestant](/wiki/History_of_religion_in_the_Netherlands "History of religion in the Netherlands"), Fleming and Walloon, he skillfully worked to exploit these divisions. By this means, he regained the allegiance of the Walloon provinces for the king. Through the [treaty of Arras](/wiki/Union_of_Arras "Union of Arras"), January 1579, he secured the support of the "Malcontents" (the Catholic nobles of the south) for the royal cause.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020bv. II_pp. 95–149-16) The rebels in the seven northern provinces then formed the [Union of Utrecht](/wiki/Union_of_Utrecht "Union of Utrecht"), formally abjuring Phillip's rule and pledging to fight to the end.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChisholm1911184-7)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Inname_van_Maastricht_door_de_hertog_van_Parma%2C_1579_Expugnacion_De_Mastric_%28titel_op_object%29%2C_RP-P-OB-79.816.jpg/250px-Inname_van_Maastricht_door_de_hertog_van_Parma%2C_1579_Expugnacion_De_Mastric_%28titel_op_object%29%2C_RP-P-OB-79.816.jpg)](/wiki/File:Inname_van_Maastricht_door_de_hertog_van_Parma,_1579_Expugnacion_De_Mastric_(titel_op_object),_RP-P-OB-79.816.jpg)

The [Siege of Maastricht](/wiki/Siege_of_Maastricht_(1579) "Siege of Maastricht (1579)") in 1579

As soon as he had secured a base of operations in [Hainaut](/wiki/County_of_Hainaut "County of Hainaut") and [Artois](/wiki/Artois "Artois"), Farnese set himself in earnest to the task of reconquering [Brabant](/wiki/Duchy_of_Brabant "Duchy of Brabant") and [Flanders](/wiki/Flanders "Flanders") by force of arms, beginning with [Maastricht](/wiki/Maastricht "Maastricht"). Farnese commenced the [Siege of Maastricht](/wiki/Siege_of_Maastricht_(1579) "Siege of Maastricht (1579)") on 12 March 1579. He ordered his troops to [sap](/wiki/Sapping "Sapping") the walls. The inhabitants of Maastricht were also digging to reach the Spanish tunnels. Deep underground the fighting continued, hundreds of Spanish soldiers died as boiling oil was poured into their tunnels. Others died because of a lack of oxygen when the Dutch defenders ignited fires within them. Another 500 Spanish soldiers died when a mine, which they planned to use to blow up the wall, exploded prematurely.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

On the night of 29 June, Farnese's men managed to get into the city while the exhausted defenders were asleep. Since the city had not surrendered after the walls had been breached, laws of warfare in the 16th century gave the victors the right to loot the conquered city. The Spanish looted the city for three days during which time many civilians lost their lives. The looting was particularly violent, perhaps because Farnese was in bed with a fever during those three days.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020bv. II_pp. 150–195-18)

In adherence to the treaty of Arras, the Spanish troops were expelled from the country so Alexander only had the Walloon troops available for the [Siege of Tournai (Dutch)](https://nl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Beleg_van_Doornik_(1581) "nl:Beleg van Doornik (1581)"). The difficulties encountered with the ragtag troops during that siege helped convince the Walloon lords to allow for the return of foreign, more importantly, Spanish troops.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020cv. III_pp. 9–55-19)

In a war composed mostly of sieges rather than battles, Parma proved his mettle. His strategy was to offer generous terms for surrender: there would be no massacres or looting; historic urban privileges were retained; there was a full pardon and amnesty; return to the Catholic Church would be gradual.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Parma%27s_brug%2C_Antwerpen_1585.JPG/250px-Parma%27s_brug%2C_Antwerpen_1585.JPG)](/wiki/File:Parma%27s_brug,_Antwerpen_1585.JPG)

Parma's bridge-of-boats over the Scheldt in 1585.

The apex of Alexander Farnese's career was when he laid [siege to the great seaport of Antwerp](/wiki/Fall_of_Antwerp "Fall of Antwerp").[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020dv. IV-21) The town was open to the sea, strongly fortified, and defended with resolute determination and courage by its citizens. The latter were led by the famous [Marnix van St. Aldegonde](/wiki/Marnix_van_St._Aldegonde "Marnix van St. Aldegonde") and assisted by an ingenious Italian engineer named [Federigo Giambelli](/wiki/Federigo_Giambelli "Federigo Giambelli"). The siege began in 1584 and called forth all of Farnese's military genius. He cut off all access to Antwerp from the sea by constructing a [bridge of boats](/wiki/Bridge_of_boats "Bridge of boats") across the [Scheldt](/wiki/Scheldt "Scheldt") from [Kallo](/wiki/Kallo "Kallo") (then spelt *Calloo*) to Oordam, in spite of the desperate efforts of the besieged townspeople.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChisholm1911184-7) The terms offered included the clause that all Protestants had to leave the city within four years. This disciplined capture and occupation of the town should not be confused with the bloody events of the [Spanish Fury](/wiki/Spanish_Fury "Spanish Fury") on 4 November 1576. Farnese avoided the mistakes of his predecessors including [Don Luis de Requesens](/wiki/Don_Luis_de_Requesens "Don Luis de Requesens").[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020av. I_pp. vii–viii-22)[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020bv. II_p. 46-23)[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020cv. III_pp. 142–143-24)[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020dv. IV_p. 24-25) With the [Fall of Antwerp](/wiki/Fall_of_Antwerp "Fall of Antwerp"), and with [Mechelen](/wiki/Mechelen "Mechelen") and Brussels already in the hands of Farnese, the whole of the southern Netherlands was once more placed under the authority of Philip II. Both [Holland](/wiki/Holland "Holland") and [Zeeland](/wiki/Zeeland "Zeeland"), whose geographical position made them unassailable except by water, were hard-pressed to retain the territory.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Tachtigjarigeoorlog-1585.png/250px-Tachtigjarigeoorlog-1585.png)](/wiki/File:Tachtigjarigeoorlog-1585.png)

State of war in 1585.

Alexander pressed operations in the regions of the Meuse and the Rhine so as to maintain trade with Germany and prepare a gateway for winning Holland and Zeeland.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_pp. 30–71-26) Unfortunately for the prince, Phillip II's parsimonious disbursement of money started having its effect in the campaigns following Antwerp's conquest. The first notable Spanish defeat under Farnese's command came at his first attempt to take control of [Grave](/wiki/Grave,_Netherlands "Grave, Netherlands"). In December 1585, with the growing food shortage, Farnese marched his troops towards the Rhine and Meuse regions so as to spare Flanders, Brabant and the Walloon provinces the burden of feeding them and, while there, undertake operations to secure trade along those rivers.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_p. 26-27) That winter was nearly disastrous for Farnese's army were it not for the ["Miracle of Empel"](/wiki/Battle_of_Empel "Battle of Empel").[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_pp. 26–28-28) Nevertheless, by 7 June the [Siege of Grave (1586)](/wiki/Siege_of_Grave_(1586) "Siege of Grave (1586)") was a fait accompli.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_pp. 30–43-29) On the other hand, fortunately for Alexander, who became Duke upon his father's death in 1586, the poorly supplied English forces, sent by Elizabeth I, were duly defeated by the Duke's forces.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChisholm1911184-7) The [Siege of Sluis (1587)](/wiki/Siege_of_Sluis_(1587) "Siege of Sluis (1587)") was necessary, so as to secure a safe harbour for the Armada ships, and was successful.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_pp. 127–139-30)

## Spanish Armada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alexander_Farnese,_Duke_of_Parma&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Spanish Armada")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Invincible_Armada.jpg/250px-Invincible_Armada.jpg)](/wiki/File:Invincible_Armada.jpg)

Alexander Farnese was made responsible for the failure of the Armada which marked the beginning of the end of his brilliant career.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_p. 230–242-31)

When Alexander Farnese became [Duke of Parma](/wiki/List_of_Dukes_of_Parma "List of Dukes of Parma") through the death of his father; he never ruled, instead naming his son Ranuccio as regent. He applied for leave to visit his paternal territory but Philip II would not grant it as there were no suitable candidates in the Netherlands to replace him. However, while retaining him in his command at the head of a formidable army, the king would not give his sanction to his great general's desire to use it for the conquest of England,[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChisholm1911184-7) at the time a supporter of the rebels.

Although Farnese was not enthusiastic about the project, in November 1583, he initially believed it possible to successfully invade England from the Netherlands with a force of 30,000 troops relying mainly on the hope of a native Catholic insurrection, but emphasized to Philip II that it was imperative for three conditions to be met: the main condition was the maintenance of absolute secrecy; second, secure the possession and defence of the Dutch provinces; third, keep the French from interfering either by way of a peace agreement or by sowing division amongst the Huguenots and Catholics.[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020cv. III_pp. 161–162-32)[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_p. 165–166-33) Philip overruled him and solicited the [Marquis of Santa Cruz](/wiki/%C3%81lvaro_de_Baz%C3%A1n,_Marquis_of_Santa_Cruz "Álvaro de Bazán, Marquis of Santa Cruz") to draft and present an invasion plan called the *Enterprise of England*, more commonly known as the [Spanish Armada](/wiki/Spanish_Armada "Spanish Armada"). As part of the general campaign preparations, Farnese moved against [Ostend](/wiki/Ostend "Ostend") and [Sluis](/wiki/Sluis "Sluis"), the latter of which would be taken on August 1587.

The plan was that Parma's troops would cross the channel in barges, protected by the Armada. Santa Cruz was appointed commander of the armada but died in early 1588 and command of the armada was given to the incompetent [Duke of Medina Sidonia](/wiki/Alonso_de_Guzm%C3%A1n_y_Sotomayor,_7th_Duke_of_Medina_Sidonia "Alonso de Guzmán y Sotomayor, 7th Duke of Medina Sidonia"). The Armada entered the English Channel in the summer of that year but poor communication between Parma and the Armada's commander made effective coordination difficult. Alexander informed Philip II that his barges were nothing more than flat-bottomed transport vessels, not warships, and he was being blockaded by English ships thus preventing him from leaving Nieuwpoort and Dunkirk. Farnese expected the Armada to clear a passage for his barges. Parma's troops were also threatened by the presence of Dutch forces in [flyboats](/wiki/Flyboat "Flyboat"), who hoped to destroy the barges and drown Parma's army at sea. In contrast, Medina Sidonia expected Parma to fight his way out from the ports and meet him in the channel.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_pp. 160–228-34) The English attack on the Armada in the [Battle of Gravelines (1588)](/wiki/Spanish_Armada#Battle_of_Gravelines "Spanish Armada"), followed by an unfavourable change in wind direction, made link-up impossible.

After the failure of the Armada, fortune seems to have abandoned the Duke of Parma.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_p. 242-35) Farnese broke up his camp in Dunkirk in September and sent the [Marquis de Renty](/wiki/Emanuel_Philibert_de_Lalaing "Emanuel Philibert de Lalaing") to the island of [Tholen](/wiki/Tholen "Tholen")[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_p. 243-36) in preparation to besiege the predominately English garrison at [Bergen Op Zoom](/wiki/Bergen_Op_Zoom "Bergen Op Zoom"). Renty did not succeed in capturing Tholen, blaming bad weather, whereupon Farnese expressed that he should have led the expedition personally.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_p. 244-37) Nevertheless, on 19 September 1588, Alexander set out from Bruges with his army to besiege Bergen Op Zoom. After [a six-week siege](/wiki/Siege_of_Bergen_op_Zoom_(1588) "Siege of Bergen op Zoom (1588)"), with winter approaching, Parma abandoned the enterprise and withdrew to Brussels, sending his troops into winter quarters.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_p. 246-38)

Alexander's final major victory in the Netherlands was [Geertruidenberg](/wiki/Geertruidenberg "Geertruidenberg"), a strategic gateway to Holland. The English garrison there was in full revolt for lack of pay. An English representative [offered the city](/wiki/Capture_of_Geertruidenberg_(1589) "Capture of Geertruidenberg (1589)") to Parma and it was ultimately delivered to him on 9 April 1589.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_pp. 253–255-39)

## French Wars of Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alexander_Farnese,_Duke_of_Parma&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: French Wars of Religion")]

The Duke of Parma started feeling the first effects of oedema after the failed siege of Bergen op Zoom.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_p. 249-40) He had to go to the [town of Spa](/wiki/Spa,_Belgium "Spa, Belgium") to treat his illness for nearly six months.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_pp. 256–266-41) During this time, the [Old Tercio of Lombardy](https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tercio_Viejo_de_Lombard%C3%ADa "es:Tercio Viejo de Lombardía") had mutinied and Farnese ordered that it be dissolved.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_pp. 265–269-42) Following this incident, Alexander's lieutenants suffered defeats in [Friesland](/wiki/Friesland "Friesland") and [Rheinberg](/wiki/Rheinberg "Rheinberg").[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_p. 269-43)

### First expedition into France

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alexander_Farnese,_Duke_of_Parma&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: First expedition into France")]

Farnese intended to turn his attention back to the northern Netherlands, where the Dutch rebels had regrouped, but on the night of 1–2 August 1589, [Henry III of France](/wiki/Henry_III_of_France "Henry III of France") was assassinated, and Farnese was ordered into France, in support of the [Duke of Mayenne](/wiki/Charles,_Duke_of_Mayenne "Charles, Duke of Mayenne") and the Catholic opposition to Protestant [Henri de Navarre](/wiki/Henry_IV_of_France "Henry IV of France") known also as the "Béarnaise".[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_pp. 275–286-44) This enabled the Dutch rebels to regain momentum in their [revolt](/wiki/Dutch_Revolt "Dutch Revolt"), which had been in ever deeper trouble since 1576. Parma had warned Philip II that the French incursion would endanger the gains made in the Netherlands and stated he would not accept responsibility for the losses or failures resulting from not heeding his advice.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_p. 290-45)

Parma left Brussels on 6 August 1590 and ultimately arrived at [Guise](/wiki/Guise "Guise") on 15 August.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_p. 293-46) In late August, he moved to relieve Paris from the [lengthy siege](/wiki/Siege_of_Paris_(1590) "Siege of Paris (1590)") it had been placed under by [Huguenots](/wiki/Huguenot "Huguenot") and [Royalists](/wiki/Royalists "Royalists") loyal to Henry IV. Farnese's main goal was simply to resupply Paris by raising the blockade, not obliterating Henry's army.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_pp. 295–297-47) When Henry learned of the approach of Mayenne and Farnese, he broke camp to actively engage in battle.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_p. 297-48) Parma had no intention of engaging in combat. He determined that capturing the fort at [Lagny-sur-Marne](/wiki/Lagny-sur-Marne "Lagny-sur-Marne") would ensure traffic was maintained along the river [Marne](/wiki/Marne_(river) "Marne (river)") which was still in the hands of the [Catholic League](/wiki/Catholic_League_(French) "Catholic League (French)").[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_p. 297-48) At dawn on 5 September, Lagny was bombarded then stormed by Spanish troops who put its 800-man garrison to the sword, all within sight of Henri's camp, a mere 12 km away.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_pp. 297–299-49) The latter abandoned the siege of Paris two days later but made one last "[Hail Mary](/wiki/Hail_Mary_pass#Metaphorical_usage_in_other_fields "Hail Mary pass")" attempt on 8–9 September which failed.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_p. 299-50) With the course of the Marne completely open to traffic, supplies flowed into Paris for the next several days.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_p. 300-51)

Keeping Paris supplied required the influx of victuals from multiple sources but most of Henri's forces occupied the areas along the [Seine](/wiki/Seine "Seine") and [Yonne](/wiki/Yonne_(river) "Yonne (river)") rivers so Parma decided to clear [Corbeil](/wiki/Corbeil-Essonnes "Corbeil-Essonnes") in order to restore traffic on the Seine. The siege began on 22 September and by 16 October, the town was taken. Its garrison was put to the sword and the town was thoroughly sacked.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_p. 306-52) With the siege of Paris lifted and its supply routes secured, Farnese took the road on 3 November back to the Netherlands, where [Maurice of Nassau](/wiki/Maurice,_Prince_of_Orange "Maurice, Prince of Orange") had gone on the offensive.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_pp. 307–308-53) Alexander's withdraw was not an easy one. He had thousands of men, wagons and horses to move during foul weather with the Béarnaise harassing him the entire way. Anticipating these difficulties, the Duke arranged his columns in such a way that Henri could not rout him.[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_pp. 308–309-54) Twenty days into the march, on 25 November near [Amiens](/wiki/Amiens "Amiens"), Henri with his cavalry boldly charged Farnese's column only to himself be routed beyond the river [Aisne](/wiki/Aisne_(river) "Aisne (river)") and getting wounded during the retreat.[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_p. 309-55) One final unsuccessful skirmish took place on 29 November where Henri was within a few hundred paces of Farnese.[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_pp. 309–310-56)

Parma and Mayenne parted ways at Guise and Alexander arrived in Brussels on 4 December 1590.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_p. 310-57)

### Maurice of Nassau's offensive

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alexander_Farnese,_Duke_of_Parma&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Maurice of Nassau's offensive")]

Alexander Farnese had appointed [Peter Ernst von Mansfeld](/wiki/Ernst_von_Mansfeld "Ernst von Mansfeld") as acting Governor-General while he was in France. Days after Farnese's departure for France, Mansfeld and Colonel [Francisco Verdugo](/wiki/Francisco_Verdugo "Francisco Verdugo"), operating in Friesland, began lamenting to Philip II about the lack of money, supplies, and troop mutinies, all the things Farnese had been complaining about for the past several years.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_pp. 311–312-58) By the time Farnese had returned, Maurice had regained Steenbergen, Roosendaal, Oosterhout, Turnhout, and Westerlo. Farnese's absence brought with it a reduction in military activity on the part of the Spanish thus allowing the Dutch some time to reflect on what policies they needed to adopt that would be most effective against their enemy. This was the beginning of the [Dutch Military Reforms](/wiki/Military_Revolution "Military Revolution") which finally allowed them to have an even footing against the Spanish; Alexander had finally met his match in Maurice.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_pp. 313–320-59)

### Second expedition into France

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alexander_Farnese,_Duke_of_Parma&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Second expedition into France")]

On the night of 24 July 1591, just days after engaging in the [siege of Knodsenburg](/wiki/Siege_of_Knodsenburg "Siege of Knodsenburg"), Alexander Farnese received orders from Philip II to drop everything and go back to France to aid the Catholic League.[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_pp. 320–321-60) Realizing the difficulties of capturing this fort, he was actually relieved to be able to honourably abandon this enterprise engaged under bad auspices. Before he could even consider another expedition to aid the League, he needed to resume his treatments at Spa whence he arrived on 1 August with his son [Ranuccio](/wiki/Ranuccio_I_Farnese,_Duke_of_Parma "Ranuccio I Farnese, Duke of Parma").[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_p. 323-61) In mid-November, Alexander drafted instructions for the interim Governor-General, Mansfeld again, in addition to putting measures in place for the defence of the Netherlands while he was away.[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_pp. 331–332-62) By the end of November, the Duke was in [Valenciennes](/wiki/Valenciennes "Valenciennes") where he mustered his troops.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_p. 333-63) Towards the middle of January 1592, Parma had rendezvoused with Mayenne and made preparations to [rescue Rouen](/wiki/Siege_of_Rouen_(1591) "Siege of Rouen (1591)") from Henri.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_p. 337-64)

Before heading to Rouen, Farnese made the strategic decision to capture [Neufchâtel-en-Bray](/wiki/Neufch%C3%A2tel-en-Bray "Neufchâtel-en-Bray").[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_p. 343-65) This would keep supply lines open. Finally, on 20 April, Parma arrived a few miles from Rouen where he was met by 50 cavalry sent by [Villars](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_de_Brancas "André de Brancas"). They informed him that Henri had lifted the siege and withdrew in the direction of [Pont-de-l'Arche](/wiki/Pont-de-l%27Arche "Pont-de-l'Arche") to entrench there.;[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_pp. 346–348-66) Rouen was saved. Rather than follow Farnese's advice and attack Henri's camp and destroy his forces, the League's leaders chose to capture [Caudebec-en-Caux](/wiki/Siege_of_Caudebec "Siege of Caudebec") where he was subsequently wounded by a musket shot in the right forearm during the siege whilst reconnoitring the town.[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_p. 349-67) The wound undermined his already precarious health even more and he was forced to call his son Ranuccio to take command of the troops. Henri saw therein an opportunity to avenge the loss of Rouen. Rather than risk a full attack against the League's forces, he took a page from Farnese's book and decided to cut off all supply routes and starve them.[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_p. 350-68) The League's army abandoned Caudebec for [Yvetot](/wiki/Yvetot "Yvetot").[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_p. 350-68) The situation was worse than at Caudebec, all the while, Farnese was seriously ill and mostly bed-ridden yet still sharp in mind. The Duke of Parma finally devised a plan to clandestinely cross the Seine in boats leaving just enough men to make Henri believe the entire army was encamped. The Catholic army was across the river and long gone by the time Henri learned of it, literally right under his nose.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_pp. 352–354-69) After returning to the Netherlands, Alexander received a letter from [Pope Clement VIII](/wiki/Pope_Clement_VIII "Pope Clement VIII") on 28 June congratulating him “for rescuing the Catholic Army.”[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_p. 355-70) Farnese quickly made his way back to Spa for yet more treatments.

## Death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alexander_Farnese,_Duke_of_Parma&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Death")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Print_Baudartius_funeral_procession_Duke_of_Parma.jpg/250px-Print_Baudartius_funeral_procession_Duke_of_Parma.jpg)](/wiki/File:Print_Baudartius_funeral_procession_Duke_of_Parma.jpg)

Arrival of the funeral procession of the Duke of Parma in Brussels 1592. Print from 'The Wars of Nassau' by [Willem Baudartius](/wiki/Willem_Baudartius "Willem Baudartius").

### Alexander Farnese's removal from office

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alexander_Farnese,_Duke_of_Parma&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Alexander Farnese's removal from office")]

Ever since the failed armada campaign against England, Spanish agents and courtiers in Philip II's court who were jealous of Farnese's success had been engaged in a malicious campaign to discredit the Duke of Parma in the eyes of the king. After Farnese's return from his second French campaign, the king, who had always favoured his nephew Alexander, allowed these complaints and accusations to influence his opinion. This change in sentiment caused the king to order the duke removed from his post in the Netherlands.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_pp. 370–372-71) The sovereign drafted a recall letter on 20 February 1592, while Farnese was marching on Rouen, and tasked Juan Pacheco de Toledo, [II marqués de Cerralbo](https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/Marquesado_de_Cerralbo "es:Marquesado de Cerralbo"), to personally deliver it to the duke upon his return to Flanders. Cerrablo died along the way.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_pp. 373–375-72) The king then assigned [Pedro Henriquez de Acevedo, Count of Fuentes](/wiki/Pedro_Henriquez_de_Acevedo,_Count_of_Fuentes "Pedro Henriquez de Acevedo, Count of Fuentes") to carry out this mission. The date of the recall letter was changed to 28 June 1592. Given Philip II's nature toward duplicitous intrigue, the king reassured Farnese that everything was fine, all the while arranging for his recall from Flanders.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_p. 375-73) The duke knew nothing about these machinations and by October was feeling well enough to return to Brussels only to learn that he was ordered to aid the League yet again.

### Final expedition into France

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alexander_Farnese,_Duke_of_Parma&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Final expedition into France")]

Fully aware of his state of health, the Duke of Parma, nevertheless, prepared for this campaign by arranging for loans and sumptuous quarters in Paris so as to give the appearance of a powerful representative of the King of Spain.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_p. 378-74) He had written his last will and testament, repeatedly went to confession and took holy communion, and sent his son back to Parma so that, upon his death, the Farnesian States would not be deprived of a ruler.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_pp. 378–379-75)

The duke left Brussels on 11 November, arriving in Arras where, on 2 December 1592, he died at the age of 47.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2020ev. V_pp. 381–383-76)

His mortal remains were dressed in a Capuchin habit, moved to Parma and buried in the Capuchin church, next to his wife's tomb. Later, their mortal remains were moved to the crypt of the Basilica of the Madonna della Steccata, where they are still found today. His death spared him from seeing the provision by which he was relieved of the post of Governor-General.[[77]](#cite_note-77)

In January 2020, the Duke's remains were exhumed in a bid to clarify the circumstances of his death which were ultimately determined to be pneumonia.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarek_y_Villarino_de_Brugge2021Addendum _p. 266-78)

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alexander_Farnese,_Duke_of_Parma&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Legacy")]

Historian [Edward Shepherd Creasy](/wiki/Edward_Shepherd_Creasy "Edward Shepherd Creasy") regarded Farnese as "one of the most formidable generals that ever could be placed at the head of an army designed not only to win battles, but to effect conquests" due to his combination of military talent and political wisdom.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECreasy1851351-1) Others authors similarly cite him as the first general of his age.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDe_Carlos_Bertrán20188-79)[[80]](#cite_note-80) Historian [John Keegan](/wiki/John_Keegan "John Keegan") extolled Farnese's military skill and his conjunction of "Italian cunning... and love of battle".[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeeganWheatcroft2014229–231-81) [Garrett Mattingly](/wiki/Garrett_Mattingly "Garrett Mattingly") also praised his ability to fluidly coordinate the many nationalities and allegiances of the Spanish armies.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGriffin2012128-2) His name is commemorated in the modern [Tercio Alejandro Farnesio No. 4](/wiki/Tercio_%22Alejandro_Farnesio%22_No._4_of_the_Legion "Tercio \"Alejandro Farnesio\" No. 4 of the Legion") of the [Spanish Legion](/wiki/Spanish_Legion "Spanish Legion").

[Spencer C. Tucker](/wiki/Spencer_C._Tucker "Spencer C. Tucker") also considered Farnese as "a brilliant strategist and diplomat and an able military commander", under whom Spain achieved the most comprehensive successes in the history of the [Eighty Years' War](/wiki/Eighty_Years%27_War "Eighty Years' War"), capturing more than thirty towns between 1581 and 1587 before being diverted to the French theater.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETucker2009821-822-82) Farnese was esteemed even by his Protestants adversaries for his success and charisma, as well as his good faith with the vanquished.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGriffin2012128-2) He found his only rival in [Maurice of Nassau](/wiki/Maurice,_Prince_of_Orange "Maurice, Prince of Orange"), who Keegan considered probably inferior to Farnese.[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeeganWheatcroft2014229–231-81) Farnese's campaigns are considered the original source of the separation between the modern nations of [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium") and the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands").[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Groof1993195-219-3)[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGriffin2012128-2)

### Illegitimate issue

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alexander_Farnese,_Duke_of_Parma&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Illegitimate issue")]

Alessandro Farnese had an affair with Françoise de Renty (a.k.a., "La Belle Franchine"), a young [Flemish](/wiki/County_of_Flanders "County of Flanders") noblewoman, confirmed by an unpublished letter of instruction from Parma, contained in the manuscript at the Bibliotecca Nazionale di Napoli, Brancacciani F1, ff. 68–91v, to Pietro Caetani, on his way to serve Alessandro Farnese in the Netherlands, on how he should behave with her:

> *Ama il Principe una Signora di qualità e fa piacere che da coloro che stimano il favor suo ella che sia corteggiata e servita…* [The Prince loves a lady of quality and is pleased when she is courted and served by those who esteem his favour...]

Although there is nothing to say with certainty about children from this relationship, according to Bertini,[[83]](#cite_note-83) Farnese arranged for, and gave incentives to,[[84]](#cite_note-84) Count Jean-Charles de Gavre, a nobleman in Alessandro's household, to marry Françoise in 1586. Considering that the couple's first child, Marie-Alexandrine-Françoise de Gavre, was born soon afterwards in 1587, that her name (Alexandrine) is not found amongst either parent's ancestors, and the influence Françoise had over Farnese,[[85]](#cite_note-85) it is quite possible that he was the child's biological father.
